Mei was three years old when she first knew she was different. It was her first day at preschool in Toronto. All the other children were white. They all spoke English. Mei had dark hair and brown eyes. She spoke Cantonese at home with her grandfather. When her grandfather tried to leave the preschool that morning, Mei threw a terrible fit. She screamed and cried and held onto his leg. She knew if he left, she would be alone. Alone with her difference.

Her grandfather stayed. Every single day for two years, he stayed at preschool with her. He could not speak a word of English. But he was there. Because Mei was terrified to be by herself in a place where everyone else looked different, sounded different, belonged in a way she did not.

Mei's parents had immigrated to Canada before she was born. They came from a small village in southern China with almost no money. They met in English class. They married in a small office with no wedding because they needed to save every dollar for the future. Both of them worked full time. Her mother cleaned office buildings at night. Her father worked in a restaurant kitchen during the day. They left before Mei woke up. They came home after she was asleep. So her grandfather took care of Mei and her little brother.

When Mei started elementary school, she only had two friends. Their names were Amy and Catherine. They were the only other Asian children in her entire grade. Mei felt safe with them. They understood. But she did not feel comfortable with them, either. She felt like they knew something about being Chinese that she did not know. They spoke Mandarin or Cantonese at home. Mei's parents had decided not to teach her. They wanted her to focus on English. To fit in. To succeed in Canada.

At school, Mei watched the other children carefully. She noticed what they wore. What they ate for lunch. How they talked. She wanted to copy them perfectly. When her mother suggested packing rice and vegetables for lunch, Mei refused. She asked for sandwiches. Plain sandwiches with cheese or peanut butter. Nothing that smelled different. Nothing that would make the other children stare.

At home, Mei saw how her parents changed. At the dinner table, they spoke in their normal voices. They laughed loudly. They told stories in Cantonese with their hands moving quickly through the air. But when the phone rang and it was her father's boss, everything changed. Her father's voice became quieter. Softer. More careful. He used simpler words. He apologized for things that were not his fault. When he hung up the phone, Mei asked him why he talked that way.

"It is easier," he said. But Mei saw the shame in his eyes.

One day, Mei went with her parents to buy a new washing machine. The salesman looked at Mei's parents and then looked away. He spoke to Mei instead. He asked her what her parents wanted. He asked her if they understood him. Mei felt her face get hot. She felt embarrassed. Not for herself. For her parents. Because they were standing right there. Because they spoke English. Because the salesman was treating them like they were invisible.

Her mother wore traditional Chinese clothing sometimes. Long dresses with beautiful embroidery. When they went out in public, people stared. Mei started to hate those dresses. She wanted her mother to wear jeans and T-shirts like the other mothers. She wanted her parents to speak without accents. She wanted them to blend in. Because when they did not blend in, neither did she.

In middle school, Mei made a decision. She decided to pull away from anything that marked her as different. She stopped speaking Cantonese, even the little bit she knew. She refused to go to Chinese school on Saturdays. She spent all her time with white friends. She joined the volleyball team. She got good grades in English and stopped trying as hard in math, because everyone expected Asian kids to be good at math and Mei wanted to be different from that expectation.

Her friends started calling her whitewashed. They said, "We don't even think of you as Asian sometimes." Mei felt proud when they said this. She called herself a banana. Yellow on the outside. White on the inside. It was a joke. But it was also the truth of what she wanted to be.

When Mei was thirteen, she started delivering newspapers to earn money. She was putting a paper into a mailbox when a man came out of his house. He shook his head at her.

"Don't you want the paper?" Mei asked politely.

The man shook his head again and waved her away.

The next week, an old woman was waiting for Mei at the same house.

"He doesn't want you to deliver his paper," the woman said quietly. "He doesn't like Chinese people. But I still want it."

Mei stood there frozen. She felt like someone had punched her in the stomach. She wanted to cry. But more than that, she felt angry. Angry at the man. Angry at herself. Because she had been trying so hard to be different from who she was. And it did not matter. To that man, she would always be Chinese. She would always be yellow on the outside. It did not matter what color she was on the inside.

In high school, Mei took a history class. They studied immigration to Canada in the 1920s. The textbook showed old newspaper cartoons. They showed Chinese people as evil. As a threat. They showed signs that said "No Chinese Allowed." The teacher talked about how Chinese workers were paid less than white workers for the same job. How they were blamed for taking jobs. How they were hated.

Mei looked at the cartoons and felt sick. She thought about the newspaper man who did not like Chinese people. She thought about her father's boss talking down to him on the phone. She thought about the salesman who would not look at her parents. She realized that nothing had really changed. The hatred was just quieter now. More polite. But still there.

She looked around her history classroom. She was the only Asian student. All her white friends were sighing and shaking their heads at how terrible racism was in the past. But they did not see that it was still happening. Right in front of them. To Mei. To her parents. Every single day.

That night, Mei went home and looked in the mirror. She had spent years trying to be less Chinese. Less different. But looking at her face, she knew the truth. She would never not be Chinese. No matter how perfect her English was. No matter how many white friends she had. No matter what.

She thought about her grandfather. How he had stayed at preschool with her every day for two years. How he had never learned English but had never stopped being exactly who he was. She thought about her parents. How they worked themselves to death to give her opportunities. How they had given up everything. Their language. Their home. Their culture. All for her.

And she felt ashamed. Not of them. Of herself.

When Mei went to university, everything changed. On her first day, she was sitting alone in the cafeteria when a group of Asian students walked up to her. They asked if she wanted to sit with them. At first, Mei wanted to say no. Old habits. But something made her say yes.

They talked about their families. About growing up caught between two worlds. About feeling too white for their Asian relatives and too Asian for their white friends. About their parents' accents and their mothers' traditional food and the shame they had felt as children. About the racism they pretended not to notice. About the jokes they laughed at even when they hurt.

For the first time in her life, Mei did not have to explain herself. Everyone at the table understood. They had all lived it. They all carried the same weight. The same confusion about where they belonged.

One girl said, "I used to call myself a banana. I was so proud of being whitewashed." Everyone laughed. Because they had all done the same thing. They had all tried to erase parts of themselves. Parts that would not erase no matter how hard they tried.

Mei started spending more time with these friends. She started asking her parents to teach her Cantonese again. She went to the library and checked out books about Chinese history and culture. She signed up for a Mandarin class. She started cooking traditional foods with her mother. She even bought her mother flowers one day and told her the embroidered dresses were beautiful.

Her mother cried.

In her second year of university, Mei had to give a presentation for her art class. When it was her turn, she stood up in front of everyone. And for the first time in her entire life, she introduced herself the right way.

"My name is Mei," she said. Not the Americanized pronunciation she had always used. But the real way. The Chinese way. The way her grandfather had always said it.

Some people in the class looked confused. But Mei did not care. She was claiming something that had always belonged to her. Her name. Her identity. Her place in the world.

After class, a white student came up to her. "I couldn't pronounce your name before," he said. "Can you teach me the right way?"

Mei smiled. "Of course."

She realized something important that day. She did not have to choose between being Chinese and being Canadian. She did not have to be one or the other. She could be both. She could be something new. Something third. A bridge between two worlds.

She would never fully belong in China. She was born in Canada. She thought in English. She had never lived in her parents' village. But she would also never fully belong in the white Canadian world. Her face would always mark her as different. Her family history would always be different.

And that was okay. Because she was creating her own culture. A third culture. One that took the best from both worlds. The strength and sacrifice of her parents. The independence and opportunity of Canada. The food and language and history of China. The diversity and openness of Toronto.

She was not yellow on the outside and white on the inside. She was not a banana anymore. She was Mei. Fully Chinese. Fully Canadian. Fully herself.

When she went home that weekend, she told her father she wanted to hear stories about his childhood in China. He looked surprised. Then his eyes filled with tears. He began to talk. In Cantonese and English mixed together. About rice fields and his own grandfather and leaving home with almost nothing.

Mei listened. Really listened. And she felt something she had not felt in a very long time. Pride. Not shame. Not embarrassment. But deep, complete pride in where she came from. In who her parents were. In who she was becoming.

Her mother came and sat down. She brought tea and almond cookies. The three of them sat together as the sun set outside the window. Two languages mixing in the air. Two cultures meeting in the same room. In the same family. In the same person.

Mei finally understood what her grandfather had known all along. You do not have to choose. You do not have to erase parts of yourself to belong. You belong to yourself. And that is enough.
